topic: European Powers & Early Conflicts in India (1498–1757)

questions.text: Who was the first European to discover the sea route to India?
questions.explanation: Vasco da Gama, a Portuguese explorer, was the first European to reach India by sea. He arrived at Calicut (Kozhikode) on the Malabar Coast on 20 May 1498, after sailing around the Cape of Good Hope. This discovery broke the Arab monopoly on the spice trade and opened the age of European commerce in Asia. Christopher Columbus reached the Americas in 1492 while looking for India — he never arrived in India.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Vasco da Gama as first European to reach India by sea is one of the most standard Modern History questions

questions.text: The Battle of Wandiwash (1760) was fought between which two European powers?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Wandiwash (22 January 1760) was fought between the British (under Sir Eyre Coote) and the French (under Count de Lally) during the Third Carnatic War. The British decisively defeated the French, ending French military power in India. This was the culmination of the Anglo-French rivalry in India that had played out through three Carnatic Wars (1746–1763). After Wandiwash, the British captured Pondicherry (1761) and became the dominant European power in peninsular India.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Battle of Wandiwash as the decisive Anglo-French battle in India is a frequently tested Modern History fact

questions.text: The English East India Company was granted its Royal Charter by Queen Elizabeth I in the year:
questions.explanation: The English East India Company was founded on 31 December 1600 when Queen Elizabeth I granted it a royal charter giving it a monopoly on all English trade east of the Cape of Good Hope. The Company's first factory in India was established at Surat in 1608/1613 after receiving a trading firman from Mughal Emperor Jahangir. The Company went on to become the world's most powerful trading company and eventually the ruler of much of India.
questions.source: SSC CGL — the founding year (1600) of the English East India Company is a standard dates question

questions.text: The 'Black Hole of Calcutta' incident (1756) was associated with which Nawab of Bengal?
questions.explanation: The 'Black Hole of Calcutta' incident occurred in June 1756 when Siraj-ud-Daulah, the Nawab of Bengal who had just succeeded Alivardi Khan, captured Calcutta from the English East India Company. According to the British account (disputed by historians), 146 English prisoners were locked in a small room overnight and 123 died from suffocation and heat. Modern historians question these numbers, but the incident became British propaganda justifying their counterattack. Robert Clive subsequently retook Calcutta in January 1757.
questions.source: SSC CGL — the Black Hole of Calcutta and Siraj-ud-Daulah are standard pre-Plassey Modern History questions

questions.text: The Battle of Colachel (1741) is historically significant because:
options: It was the first battle in which the English East India Company defeated a French force
It was the first decisive defeat of a European power (Dutch) by an Indian ruler (Travancore)
It resulted in the Portuguese losing Goa to the Marathas
It ended the Second Carnatic War in favour of the French
questions.explanation: The Battle of Colachel (1741) is historically significant as the first decisive defeat of a European power — the Dutch — by an Indian ruler, Marthanda Varma, the king of Travancore. The Dutch naval commander Eustachius De Lannoy was captured and subsequently entered Travancore service, helping modernize the Travancore navy. This battle effectively ended Dutch ambitions in South India. It is sometimes called 'the first decisive Indian victory over Europeans' and contrasts sharply with the outcomes of most other European-Indian military encounters of the era.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because Battle of Colachel is an underrated fact; the 'first Indian defeat of Europeans' significance is a higher-difficulty fact that separates well-prepared students

questions.text: How did Bombay come under British control in 1661?
options: Captured from the Portuguese in a naval battle
Received as a marriage dowry when Portuguese princess Catherine of Braganza married King Charles II of England
Purchased from the Mughal Emperor Aurangzeb
Obtained by Treaty after the First Anglo-Portuguese War
questions.explanation: Bombay came under British control in 1661 not through conquest but as part of the marriage dowry when Catherine of Braganza, a Portuguese princess, married King Charles II of England. The Portuguese gave Bombay (and Tangier in North Africa) as part of the marriage settlement. Charles II subsequently leased Bombay to the English East India Company for £10 per year in 1668. This unusual diplomatic origin — a marriage gift — made Bombay unique among early British acquisitions in India.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because the 'dowry' origin of Bombay is a specific factual question; students often assume it was captured militarily

questions.text: The Siege of Arcot (1751), in which Robert Clive with only 200 soldiers held the Arcot Fort against a force of 10,000 for 53 days, took place during which Carnatic War?
options: It was not part of any Carnatic War — it was a separate conflict
questions.explanation: The Siege of Arcot (1751) took place during the Second Carnatic War (1749-1754). Arcot was the capital of the Carnatic (Nawab's seat), and Clive's bold capture and defense of it was a diversion to relieve French/Chanda Sahib pressure on Trichinopoly. The Second Carnatic War arose from succession disputes in Hyderabad (Muzaffar Jung vs Nasir Jung) and Carnatic (Chanda Sahib vs Muhammad Ali) — French backed one side, English the other. Clive's success at Arcot turned the tide in favour of the British-backed Muhammad Ali.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because the 'which Carnatic War did Arcot happen in' question catches students who remember the Arcot facts but not the war sequence

questions.text: The 'Cartaz system' introduced by the Portuguese in the Indian Ocean was:
options: A system of fixed prices for spice trade with Indian merchants
A naval pass/license that ships had to carry to trade in the Indian Ocean under Portuguese authority
A Portuguese method of collecting land taxes in Goa
A treaty system between Portugal and Indian coastal kingdoms
questions.explanation: The Cartaz was a pass or license issued by the Portuguese that all ships — Indian, Arab, Chinese, and others — had to carry while trading in the Indian Ocean. Ships without a cartaz could be seized and their cargo confiscated. This gave Portugal effective control over Indian Ocean trade without occupying large territories. It was essentially a toll system on sea lanes — unprecedented in the history of Indian Ocean trade, which had previously been open to all. The Cartaz system lasted until Dutch and English naval power eroded Portuguese dominance.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because the Cartaz system is rarely covered in standard SSC notes but appears in higher-difficulty questions about Portuguese methods in India

questions.text: The Third Carnatic War (1756-1763) was the Indian theatre of which European conflict?
questions.explanation: The Third Carnatic War (1756-1763) was the Indian extension of the Seven Years' War (1756-1763) — a global conflict involving Britain and France (as well as Prussia and Austria). The First Carnatic War (1746-1748) echoed the War of Austrian Succession (1740-1748). This pattern — European wars being fought simultaneously in India — explains why the Carnatic Wars happened when they did. The Seven Years' War ended with the Treaty of Paris (1763), which also settled the Indian conflict: France got Pondicherry back but without its fortifications.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because students know the Carnatic Wars but rarely memorise which European war each mirrors; this correlation is often tested in higher-difficulty papers

questions.text: The Dutch East India Company (VOC) was founded in 1602. What does 'VOC' stand for?
options: Vereenigde Oost-Indische Compagnie (United East India Company)
Vlaamse Oost-Indische Compagnie (Flemish East India Company)
Vrije Oost-Indische Compagnie (Free East India Company)
Vloot Oost-Indische Compagnie (Fleet East India Company)
questions.explanation: VOC stands for Vereenigde Oost-Indische Compagnie, which translates to 'United East India Company' in Dutch. It was founded in 1602 — two years after the English East India Company (1600) — and is considered the world's first multinational corporation and the first company to issue stock to the public. The VOC had sovereign powers: it could wage war, negotiate treaties, mint coins, and establish colonies. It was more powerful than the English EIC for most of the 17th century but ultimately lost the contest for India to the English.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because the full name/meaning of VOC is rarely taught; the 'first multinational corporation' fact is a good hook question

questions.text: Where did Vasco da Gama first land when he reached India in 1498?
questions.explanation: Vasco da Gama first landed at Calicut (present-day Kozhikode) on the Malabar Coast of Kerala on 20 May 1498. Calicut was then a major spice trading port ruled by the Zamorin (Samoothiri). Vasco da Gama's reception was mixed — Arab traders pressured the Zamorin against giving the Portuguese trading rights. Goa, which became the Portuguese capital in India, was only captured later by Albuquerque in 1510. This Calicut vs Goa distinction is a very common exam trap.
questions.source: Vasco da Gama's first landing point (Calicut, not Goa) is one of the most frequently tested European arrival facts in SSC CGL

questions.text: Match the following: (i) First Carnatic War | (ii) Second Carnatic War | (iii) Third Carnatic War — with their outcomes/key events:
options: (i) Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle, Madras returned to English | (ii) Dupleix recalled, Muhammad Ali became Nawab | (iii) Battle of Wandiwash, French power ended
(i) Battle of Wandiwash | (ii) Treaty of Paris | (iii) Siege of Arcot
(i) Dupleix recalled | (ii) Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle | (iii) Siege of Arcot
(i) Treaty of Paris | (ii) Battle of Wandiwash | (iii) Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle
questions.explanation: First Carnatic War (1746-48): French captured Madras; Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle returned it to English. Second Carnatic War (1749-54): Siege of Arcot by Clive; Dupleix recalled to France (1754); Muhammad Ali became Nawab with English backing. Third Carnatic War (1756-63): Battle of Wandiwash (1760) — English defeated French; Pondicherry captured (1761); Treaty of Paris (1763) ended the war — French got Pondicherry back but without fortifications. The three-war sequence with the corresponding peace treaty is the most tested Carnatic Wars pattern.
questions.source: The three Carnatic Wars — causes, key battles, and outcomes — is the most frequently tested European conflicts pattern in SSC CGL Modern History

questions.explanation: Calcutta was founded in 1690 by Job Charnock, an agent of the English East India Company who established a trading post at the villages of Sutanati, Govindpur, and Kalikata (which gave the city its name). Fort William was subsequently built there. This is different from Thomas Roe, who was the English ambassador to the Mughal court (1615-1619) and negotiated trading rights from Jahangir. Robert Clive is associated with the Battle of Plassey (1757); Warren Hastings was the first Governor-General.
questions.source: Job Charnock as founder of Calcutta is a standard 'who founded which city' pattern question in SSC CGL
